原文:

Hello coders!

This article presents a curated list of open-source starters powered by FastAPI, a trending Python framework. All mentioned products are released on GitHub and the permissive license allows using the sources in commercial projects and eLearning activities.

Thanks for reading!

✨ FastAPI Mini eCommerce

This starter built on top of a nice Bootstrap 5 design provides a simple integration of FastAPI and Stripe. The products are saved in JSON files and the UI is rendered and provisioned the information from files.

👉 FastAPI & Stripe Starter - public repository

The Stripe payment flow, in order to be functional, requires to have Stripe secrets saved in the env file when the project starts. Here is the full list of features, as mentioned in the README:

  • ✅ Tech Stack: FastAPI, Stripe
  • ✅ UI: Boostrap 5, Soft Design (free version)
  • ✅ Simple JSON persistence for products
  • ✅ Products Discovery from this directory

Once the product is up & running, we should be able to complete payment flows via Stripe and FastAPI.

✨ FastAPI Soft Dashboard

This product comes with a database, Alembic for the migration layer, API, a nice integrated UI, and unitary tests.

👉 FastAPI Soft Dashboard - source code

Soft Dashboard, the design used to style the pages, is built with over 70 frontend individual elements, like buttons, inputs, navbars, nav tabs, cards, or alerts, giving you the freedom of choosing and combining - design credits Creative-Tim.

✨ Product highlights

  • ✅ Up-to-date dependencies, Modular Codebase
  • ✅ Persistence: SQLite, MySql (production)
  • ✅ Authentication: Basic & OAuth
  • ✅ API: Products & Sales (linked tables)
  • ✅ Unitary tests

The product is under development for more features like chart integration, background tasks, and CI/CD deployment.

Items.Title: 人工智能创造音乐还有多远？ | How far off is AI created music?
Items.Description:
不久前，我发现了 AI 艺术生成器Midjourney ，并写了以下帖子：

探索这个应用程序让我想知道人工智能的未来会对音乐产生什么影响。 碰巧通过算法传递了一个 YouTube 视频，它想知道同样的问题：

除了这个视频，我还阅读了一篇关于披头士乐队最近的纪录片“回归”的文章，其中谈到了他们如何使用机器学习来增强和恢复文档中使用的 60 年代旧镜头的音频。 彼得杰克逊在采访中指出，他们能够教人工智能保罗和约翰斯的声音听起来像什么，这样他们就可以更干净地重新混合。 这是非常了不起的东西！

所以，我对你们的问题是非常开放的：

  • 你认为人工智能对音乐的未来是什么？
  • 您是否遇到过其他有关此主题的有趣资源？
  • 你认为人工智能会创作出全新的音乐，还是你认为人工智能会复制过去音乐家的风格和声音？

原文:

Not long ago, I discovered the AI art generator, Midjourney, and wrote the following post:

Exploring this app got me wondering what the future of AI might hold for music. ~Luckily~, I chanced upon was delivered via algorithm a YouTube video that wondered the same:

Along with this video, I read an article about the recent of The Beatles documentary, Get Back that talked about how they used machine learning to enhance and restore audio from the old 60's footage used in the doc. Peter Jackson noted in interview that they were able to teach the AI what Paul and Johns' voices sound like so that they could remix things more cleanly. It's pretty amazing stuff!

So, my questions to y'all are pretty open-ended:

  • What do you think the future of AI holds for music?
  • Have you come across other interesting resources talking about this subject?
  • How far off is AI created music... and what does that look like anyway? Do you think AI will be composing completely fresh music or do you think AI will be copying the styles and voices of past musicians?

Syed Mohsin Raza - Oct 17

Andressa Cabistani - Sep 30

Items.Title: 在 Astro 中将 Markdown 组件传递给 React 或 Preact | Passing Markdown components to React or Preact in Astro
Items.Description:
我正在为一个项目使用 Astro 和 Preact（与 React 具有相同的 API）。 我最近遇到了一个问题，我想将 Markdown 组件传递给项目的 Preact 端，但它没有按我预期的方式工作。

首先，我尝试将其导入到顶部的 Preact 文件中：

 // Preact Component import Blog from ' ./Blog.md ' function PreactComponent () { return ( < div > < Blog /> </ div > ) }

但遗憾的是，这并没有奏效！ 只能在.astro组件中进行这种类型的导入，即使 Astro 支持开箱即用的 Preact 和 React（以及其他功能）。

我意识到（在实际阅读文档之后，令人震惊）您不能将 Markdown 直接传递给非 Astro 组件。

所以，我认为将 Markdown 导入到父 Astro 组件中，然后从那里将其传递到我的 Preact 组件中可能会很有用。

 // Parent Astro Component --- import Blog from ' ./Blog.md ' ; --- < PreactComponent blog = { (< Blog />) } client : visible />

这很接近，但没有雪茄。 事实证明，您不能将 JSX 作为props传递给框架组件。

进入...插槽！

我在 Astro 中了解了命名插槽，这改变了游戏！ 这是一个在 Vue 和 Svelte 社区中很常见的概念，但我承认作为一个 React 人，我之前没有真正尝试过它们。

所以，我要做的是在我的 Astro 父组件中定义一个插槽：

 // Parent Astro Component --- import Blog from ' ./Blog.md ' ; --- < PreactComponent > < Blog slot = "blog" /> </ DemoContent >

瞧，我现在可以在 Astro 的 React/Preact 组件中渲染 Markdown。

这对我来说特别有用，因为我有一些非技术人员给我 Markdown 文件以在网站上共享（有一些状态驱动的逻辑决定什么时候应该显示什么副本）。 现在我能够以这种方式传递 Markdown，我可以有一个单独的 Markdown 文件夹供他们使用，而不会弄乱网站的逻辑。 呜呼！

特别感谢Ben Holmes以及Alex和Charlie帮助我解决这个问题！ Ben made an awesome little demo on StackBlitz to illustrate the issue, and here's a related Stack Overflow question as well if you find this helpful and want to upvote! Ben在 StackBlitz 上做了一个很棒的小演示来说明这个问题，如果你觉得这很有帮助并想要投票，这里还有一个相关的 Stack Overflow 问题！



原文:

I am using Astro and Preact (which has the same API as React) for a project. I ran into a issue recently where I wanted to pass a Markdown component to the Preact side of the project, and it wasn't working the way I expected.

First of all, I tried importing it into my Preact file at the top:

// Preact Component
import Blog from './Blog.md' function PreactComponent() { return ( <div> <Blog /> </div> )
}

But sadly, that didn't work! Doing that type of import is only possible in .astro components, even though Astro has support for Preact and React (amongst other things) out of the box.

I realized (after actually reading the documentation, shocking) that you can't pass Markdown directly to non-Astro components.

So, I thought it might be useful to import the Markdown into a parent Astro component, and then pass that into my Preact component from there.

// Parent Astro Component
---
import Blog from './Blog.md';
--- <PreactComponent blog={(<Blog />)} client:visible
/>

This was close, but no cigar. Turns out, you can't pass JSX as props to framework components.

Entering... slots!

I learned about Named Slots in Astro and that changed the game! This is a concept that is pretty common in the Vue and Svelte communities, but I admit as a React person I hadn't really tried them before.

So, what I had to do was define a slot in my Astro parent component:

// Parent Astro Component
---
import Blog from './Blog.md';
--- <PreactComponent> <Blog slot="blog" />
</DemoContent>

...and then from there, treat the blog slot as a prop on the Preact side!

// Preact Component function PreactComponent({ blog }) { return ( <div> {blog} </div> )
}

And voilà, I now can render Markdown in a React/Preact component in Astro.

This is particularly useful for me because I have some non-technical folks giving me Markdown files to share on a website (with some state-driven logic determining what copy should be shown when). Now that I'm able to pass in the Markdown this way, I can have a separate Markdown file folder for them to work with, without messing with the logic of the website. Woo hoo!

Special thanks to Ben Holmes for helping me out with this, as well as Alex and Charlie! Ben made an awesome little demo on StackBlitz to illustrate the issue, and here's a related Stack Overflow question as well if you find this helpful and want to upvote!
Items.Author: cassidoo

Items.Title: Execution Machine (EXM) 简介 - 永久无服务器功能 | Introduction to Execution Machine (EXM) - Permanent Serverless Functions
Items.Description:
本教程的代码位于 [这里]( https://github.com/dabit3/execution-machine

一旦数据/交易被存储，它们就可以被永久检索和使用（或者只要网络存在）。

一些有趣的用例可能是注册表、游戏、CMS、永久数据协议、一般数据处理，甚至可能构建永久 NoSQL 数据库。

在引擎盖下，EXM 利用SmartWeave

懒惰的评价

在大多数区块链中，计算和当前状态由区块链节点执行。 通过惰性评估，执行的负担转移到客户端或中间层（如 EXM），中间层实现处理器和传送器，本质上重新评估和缓存合约的最新状态，以便客户端不需要处理所有先前的交易以计算当前状态。

您可以通过他们的CLI或他们的SDK与 EXV 进行交互。 在本教程中，我们将使用JavaScript SDK与平台进行交互。

你好世界教程

让我们探索如何构建、部署和测试 EXM 应用程序。

在这个例子中，我们将创建一个基本的CMS ，它允许用户创建、更新和删除帖子。 这个例子将使我们能够实现一个基本的CRUD 应用程序，它是大多数实际应用程序的基础。

先决条件

要成功完成本教程，您必须具备：

  1. 你可以在这里得到一个
  2. 我建议通过NVM或FNM安装 Node.js
  3. 入门

    首先，我们将在一个空目录中创建一个新的 Node.js 项目：

    接下来，我们将安装我们的应用所需的依赖项：

     npm install @execution-machine/sdk uuid chai

    最后，更新package.json以添加此配置以启用 ES 模块：

    基本设置

    对于基本的 EXM 应用程序，您需要以下基本组件：

    1. 程序的初始状态
    2. 管理状态更新的处理函数。 此函数是您的 EXM 函数的入口点。 为了使写操作被认为是有效的并且能够改变函数的状态，它需要返回一个带有 state 属性的对象。 部署程序的脚本
    3. 用于读取和更新程序状态的脚本

    配置 EXM

    为了与平台交互，我们需要一些可以设置和重用的基本配置。

    首先，确保您已在此处创建了 EXM API 密钥。

    接下来，在本地配置文件或终端中创建并设置环境变量：

     export EXM_PK = <your-api-key>

    现在，创建一个名为exm.js的新文件并添加以下代码：

     /* exm.js */ import { Exm } from ' @execution-machine/sdk ' const APIKEY = process . env . EXM_PK export const exmInstance = new Exm ({ token : APIKEY })

    现在我们可以通过导入exmInstance与 EXM 进行交互。

    初始状态

    如前所述，我们将创建的应用程序将允许用户创建、编辑和删除帖子。 帖子将是具有以下字段的对象：

     type Post { id : string title : string content : string author : string }

    为了保存帖子，我们可以创建一些数据结构来保存帖子列表。 我将使用一个对象，但您也可以使用一个数组。

    要设置此初始状态，请创建一个名为state.js的新文件并添加以下代码：

     /* state.js */ export const state = { " posts " : {} }

    处理函数

    现在我们已经定义了初始状态，我们将创建处理函数。

    创建一个名为handler.js的新文件并添加以下代码：

     /* handler.js */ export async function handle ( state , action ) { const { input } = action if ( input . type === ' createPost ' || input . type === ' updatePost ' ) { state . posts [ input . post . id ] = input . post } if ( input . type === ' deletePost ' ) { delete state . posts [ input . postId ] } return { state } }

    此函数将接受三种不同类型的操作：

    如果输入type与其中任何一个都不匹配，则仅返回状态而不进行任何更新。

    部署脚本

    现在我们已经定义了状态和处理函数，我们可以创建部署脚本。

    该脚本会将我们的函数部署到 EXM 并使其可供我们开始使用。

    创建一个名为deploy.js的新文件并添加以下代码：

     /* deploy.js */ import { ContractType } from ' @execution-machine/sdk ' import fs from ' fs ' import { exmInstance } from ' ./exm.js ' import { state } from ' ./state.js ' const contractSource = fs . readFileSync ( ' handler.js ' ) const data = await exmInstance . functions . deploy ( contractSource , state , ContractType . JS ) console . log ({ data }) /* after the contract is deployed, write the function id to a local file */ fs . writeFileSync ( ' ./functionId.js ' , `export const functionId = " ${ data . id } "` )

    创建新帖子

    接下来让我们看看如何进行状态更新。

    我们要做的第一件事是创建一个新帖子。 创建一个名为createPost.js的新文件并添加以下代码：

     /* createPost.js */ import { exmInstance } from ' ./exm.js ' import { functionId } from ' ./functionId.js ' import { v4 as uuid } from ' uuid ' const id = uuid () /* the inputs array defines the data that will be available in the handler function as action.input */ const inputs = [{ type : ' createPost ' , post : { id , title : " Hello world " , content : " My first post " , author : " Nader Dabit " } }] const data = await exmInstance . functions . write ( functionId , inputs ) console . log ({ data })

    正如上面代码中所评论的，最重要的是要识别输入数组的结构，以及它们如何与handler函数中接收到的参数相关联。

    在createPost.js中， type将确定在处理程序中进行的状态更新的类型，而post是状态更新中使用的数据。

    读取当前状态

    现在我们有了部署函数和创建帖子的方法，让我们看看如何读取状态。

    为此，请创建一个名为read.js的文件并添加以下代码：

     /* read.js */ import { exmInstance } from ' ./exm.js ' import { functionId } from ' ./functionId.js ' const data = await exmInstance . functions . read ( functionId ) console . log ( " data: " , JSON . stringify ( data ))

    您还可以通过调用或访问https://api.exm.dev/read/<function-id>来读取任何函数 ID 的状态

    试一试

    现在一切都设置好了，让我们试一试吧！

    从您的终端运行部署脚本：

    部署该函数后，您应该会看到一个写入名为functionId.js的项目的新文件。

    接下来，读取程序的当前状态：

    状态的返回值应该是一个空的posts对象。

    现在，创建一个新帖子：

    然后再次读取应用程序的状态：

    返回值现在应该包括刚刚创建的新帖子。

    提醒您也可以通过调用或访问https://api.exm.dev/read/<function-id>来读取任何函数 ID 的状态

    更新和删除帖子

    现在让我们创建用于更新和删除帖子的脚本。

    要删除帖子，请创建一个名为deletePost.js的文件并添加以下代码：

     /* deletePost.js */ import { exmInstance } from ' ./exm.js ' import { functionId } from ' ./functionId.js ' const inputs = [{ type : ' deletePost ' , postId : process . argv [ 2 ] }]; await exmInstance . functions . write ( functionId , inputs )

    此脚本从运行脚本的用户那里获取一个帖子 ID 作为输入，并使用它来调用deletePost输入，并将postId作为参数传入。

    要更新帖子，请创建一个名为updatePost.js的新文件并添加以下代码：

     /* updatePost.js */ import { exmInstance } from ' ./exm.js ' import { functionId } from ' ./functionId.js ' const inputs = [{ type : ' updatePost ' , post : { id : process . argv [ 2 ], title : " Hello world V2 " , content : " My updated post! " , author : " Nader Dabit " } }] const data = await exmInstance . functions . write ( functionId , inputs ) console . log ({ data })

    现在，您应该能够通过运行这两个脚本并传入帖子 ID 来更新和删除帖子：

     node deletePost.js <post-id>

    测试

    让我们创建一个测试脚本，它将检查我们所有的操作类型是否按预期工作。

    创建一个名为test.js的新文件并添加以下代码：

     import fs from ' fs ' import { TestFunction , createWrite , FunctionType } from " @execution-machine/sdk " import { state } from ' ./state.js ' import { v4 as uuid } from ' uuid ' import { expect } from ' chai ' const id = uuid () const functionSource = fs . readFileSync ( ' ./handler.js ' ) const createPost = { type : ' createPost ' , post : { id , title : " Hello world " , content : " My first post " , author : " Nader Dabit " } } const updatePost = { type : ' updatePost ' , post : { id , title : " Hello world V2 " , content : " My updated post! " , author : " Nader Dabit " } } const deletePost = { type : ' deletePost ' , postId : id } describe ( " Testing EXM " , function () { it ( " Should create a post " , async function () { const testAttempt = await TestFunction ({ functionSource , functionType : FunctionType . JAVASCRIPT , functionInitState : state , writes : [ createWrite ( createPost )] }) const value = testAttempt . state . posts [ id ] expect ( value . id ). to . equal ( id ) expect ( value . title ). to . equal ( " Hello world " ) }) it ( " Should update a post " , async function () { const testAttempt = await TestFunction ({ functionSource , functionType : FunctionType . JAVASCRIPT , functionInitState : state , writes : [ createWrite ( createPost ), createWrite ( updatePost )] }) const value = testAttempt . state . posts [ id ] expect ( value . title ). to . equal ( " Hello world V2 " ) }) it ( " Should delete a post " , async function () { const testAttempt = await TestFunction ({ functionSource , functionType : FunctionType . JAVASCRIPT , functionInitState : state , writes : [ createWrite ( createPost ), createWrite ( deletePost )] }) const value = testAttempt . state . posts const length = Object . keys ( value ). length expect ( length ). to . equal ( 0 ) }) })

    要进行测试，请运行以下命令：

    下一步

    要了解有关 EXM 的更多信息，请查看EXM Discord 。

    要了解有关 Arweave 开发者生态系统的更多信息，请查看learn.arweave.dev 。



原文:

The code for this tutorial is located [here](https://github.com/dabit3/execution-machine

EXM is a language-agnostic serverless environment powered by Arweave, and enables developers to create permanent, serverless functions on the blockchain.

EXM eliminates the need to understand smart contracts, transactions, or blocks to leverage blockchain technology.

EXM / Arweave provide something that most traditional data stores do not - permanence. Once the data / transactions are stored, they can be retrieved and consumed forever (or as long as the network exists).

Some interesting use cases might be registries, games, CMSs, permanent data protocols, general data processing, and possibly even building a permanent NoSQL database.

Under the hood, EXM utilizes SmartWeave

Lazy Evaluation

The Smartweave (and therefore EXM) execution layer is based on what's known as "Lazy Evaluation".

In most blockchains, the computation and current state is executed by the blockchain nodes. With lazy evaluation, the burden of execution is shifted to either the client or a middle layer (like EXM) that implements a processor and conveyor that essentially re-evaluates and caches the latest state of the contract so that the client does not need to process all previous transactions in order to calculate the current state.

CLI vs SDK

You can interact with EXV either via their CLI or their SDK. In this tutorial we'll be interacting with the platform using the JavaScript SDK.

Hello World tutorial

Let's explore how to build, deploy, and test an EXM app.

In this example we'll create a basic CMS that will allow a user to create, update, and delete posts. This example will enable us to implement a basic CRUD app which is the basis for most real-world applications.

Prerequisites

To be successful with this tutorial you must have:

  1. An EXM API key. You can get one here
  2. Node.js installed on your machine. I recommend installing Node.js either via NVM or FNM
  3. Mocha installed globally (if you want to run tests)

Getting started

To get started, we'll create a new Node.js project in an empty directory:

Next, we'll install the dependencies we'll need for our app:

npm install @execution-machine/sdk uuid chai

Finally, update package.json to add this configuration to enable ES Modules:

Basic setup

For a basic EXM app you need these basic components:

  1. Initial state for the program
  2. A handler function to manage state updates. This function is the entry point for your EXM function. For a write operation to be considered valid as well as able to change the state of the function, it needs to return an object with state property in it. (more on this later)
  3. A script to deploy the program
  4. Scripts to read and update the state of the program

Configuring EXM

To interact with the platform we'll need some basic configuration that we can set up and reuse.

First, make sure you've created an EXM API key here.

Next, create and set an environment variable either in your local config file, or from your terminal:

export EXM_PK=<your-api-key>

Now, create a new file named exm.js and add the following code:

/* exm.js */
import { Exm } from '@execution-machine/sdk' const APIKEY = process.env.EXM_PK
export const exmInstance = new Exm({ token: APIKEY })

Now we'll be able to interact with EXM by importing exmInstance.

Initial State

As mentioned previously, the app we'll be creating will allow users to create, edit, and delete posts. A post will be an object with the following fields:

type Post { id: string title: string content: string author: string
}

To hold the posts, we can create some data structure to hold a list of posts. I will be using an object but you could also use an array.

To set up this initial state, create a new file named state.js and add the following code:

/* state.js */
export const state = { "posts": {}
}

Handler function

Now that we have the initial state defined we'll create the handler function.

Create a new file named handler.js and add the following code:

/* handler.js */
export async function handle(state, action) { const { input } = action if (input.type === 'createPost' || input.type === 'updatePost') { state.posts[input.post.id] = input.post } if (input.type === 'deletePost') { delete state.posts[input.postId] } return { state }
}

This function will accept three different types of actions:

createPost - Creates a new post
updatePost - Updates an existing post with new data
deletePost - Deletes a post

If the input type does not match any of these, then the state is just returned without any update.

Deploy script

Now that we have the state and the handler function defined, we can create the deploy script.

This script will deploy our function to EXM and make it available for us to start using.

Create a new file named deploy.js and add the following code:

/* deploy.js */
import { ContractType } from '@execution-machine/sdk'
import fs from 'fs'
import { exmInstance } from './exm.js'
import { state } from './state.js' const contractSource = fs.readFileSync('handler.js')
const data = await exmInstance.functions.deploy(contractSource, state, ContractType.JS) console.log({ data }) /* after the contract is deployed, write the function id to a local file */
fs.writeFileSync('./functionId.js', `export const functionId = "${data.id}"`)

Creating a new post

Next let's look at how to make a state update.

The first thing we'll want to try to do is to create a new post. Create a new file named createPost.js and add the following code:

/* createPost.js */
import { exmInstance } from './exm.js'
import { functionId } from './functionId.js'
import { v4 as uuid } from 'uuid' const id = uuid() /* the inputs array defines the data that will be available in the handler function as action.input */
const inputs = [{ type: 'createPost', post: { id, title: "Hello world", content: "My first post", author: "Nader Dabit" }
}] const data = await exmInstance.functions.write(functionId, inputs)
console.log({ data })

As commented in the code above, the most important thing to recognize is the structure of the array of inputs, and how they correlate to the arguments received in the handler function.

In createPost.js, the type will determine the type of state update that is made in the handler, and the post is the data used in the state update.

Reading the current state

Now that we have a way to deploy the function and create a post, let's look at how to read the state.

To do so, create a file named read.js and add the following code:

/* read.js */
import { exmInstance } from './exm.js'
import { functionId } from './functionId.js' const data = await exmInstance.functions.read(functionId)
console.log("data: ", JSON.stringify(data))

You can also read the state of any function ID by calling or visiting https://api.exm.dev/read/<function-id>

Trying it out

Now that everything is set up, let's give it a spin!

From your terminal, run the deploy script:

Once the function has been deployed, you should see a new file written to the project named functionId.js.

Next, read the current state of the program:

The return value of the state should be an empty posts object.

Now, create a new post:

Then read the state of the app again:

The return value should now include the new post that was just created.

Reminder that you can also read the state of any function ID by calling or visiting https://api.exm.dev/read/<function-id>

Updating and deleting posts

Let's now create the scripts for updating and deleting posts.

For deleting a post, create a file named deletePost.js and add the following code:

/* deletePost.js */
import { exmInstance } from './exm.js'
import { functionId } from './functionId.js' const inputs = [{ type: 'deletePost', postId: process.argv[2]
}]; await exmInstance.functions.write(functionId, inputs)

This script takes a post ID as input from the user running the script, and uses it to call the deletePost input, passing in the postId as an argument.

To update a post, create a new file named updatePost.js and add the following code:

/* updatePost.js */
import { exmInstance } from './exm.js'
import { functionId } from './functionId.js' const inputs = [{ type: 'updatePost', post: { id: process.argv[2], title: "Hello world V2", content: "My updated post!", author: "Nader Dabit" }
}] const data = await exmInstance.functions.write(functionId, inputs) console.log({ data })

Now, you should be able to update and delete posts by running these two scripts, passing in the post ID:

node deletePost.js <post-id>

Testing

EXM also comes built in with testing functions in their SDK to facilitate testing in a way that can be readable & efficient.

Let's create a test script that will check that all of our action types work as we expect.

Create a new file named test.js and add the following code:

import fs from 'fs'
import { TestFunction, createWrite, FunctionType } from "@execution-machine/sdk"
import { state } from './state.js'
import { v4 as uuid } from 'uuid'
import { expect } from 'chai' const id = uuid()
const functionSource = fs.readFileSync('./handler.js') const createPost = { type: 'createPost', post: { id, title: "Hello world", content: "My first post", author: "Nader Dabit" }
} const updatePost = { type: 'updatePost', post: { id, title: "Hello world V2", content: "My updated post!", author: "Nader Dabit" }
} const deletePost = { type: 'deletePost', postId: id
} describe("Testing EXM", function () { it("Should create a post", async function () { const testAttempt = await TestFunction({ functionSource, functionType: FunctionType.JAVASCRIPT, functionInitState: state, writes: [createWrite(createPost)] }) const value = testAttempt.state.posts[id] expect(value.id).to.equal(id) expect(value.title).to.equal("Hello world") }) it("Should update a post", async function () { const testAttempt = await TestFunction({ functionSource, functionType: FunctionType.JAVASCRIPT, functionInitState: state, writes: [createWrite(createPost), createWrite(updatePost)] }) const value = testAttempt.state.posts[id] expect(value.title).to.equal("Hello world V2") }) it("Should delete a post", async function () { const testAttempt = await TestFunction({ functionSource, functionType: FunctionType.JAVASCRIPT, functionInitState: state, writes: [createWrite(createPost), createWrite(deletePost)] }) const value = testAttempt.state.posts const length = Object.keys(value).length expect(length).to.equal(0) })
})

To test, run the following command:

Next steps

To learn more about EXM, check out the EXM Discord.

To learn more about the Arweave Developer Ecosystem, check out learn.arweave.dev.

Items.Title: DevOps 如何以及为何成为 DevSecOps | How and why DevOps became DevSecOps
Items.Description:
什么是 DevOps

我们先来看看什么是 DevOps（Developer Operations），这样我们就可以更好的理解为什么它现在已经演变成 DevSecOps（Developer Security Operations）了。 这可以应用于构建新产品或适用于我们今天看到的大多数产品的持续改进过程。

如果我们看一下旧的开发方式，典型的团队自己工作，会尽自己的一份力，然后将其传递给下一个团队来完成他们的工作，有点像装配线。 这意味着开发周期非常长，团队之间的沟通有限，移交流程没有到位，如果出现任何问题，识别和修复问题需要很长时间。 如果我们将其与今天的敏捷开发过程进行比较，我们会使用 DevOps 进行冲刺，并以更快、更有效的方式交付结果。 不仅通过我们看待开发周期的方式，而且通过工具和自动化，这已经成为可能。

在某些情况下，仅在流程结束时由特定团队查看安全性。

为什么将安全性集成到 DevOps 中

网络犯罪的增加和攻击的复杂性使安全成为开发过程的关键部分，而不仅仅是事后的想法。 这导致安全性被整合到每个阶段的开发过程中，并成为共同的责任。 它还导致从每个过程的开始而不是结束时就考虑和计划安全性。 这导致了更高的开发速度、更多的集成安全层以及围绕作为开发周期主要方面的安全性的更高协作。

从头到尾在每个阶段的开发周期中集成安全性，同时自动化一些安全步骤，这导致 DevOps 演变为 DevSecOps，这是当今的首选实践。 该术语被调整为包括安全性的主要原因之一是让每个人在这个过程中思考并为安全的建筑实践负责。

最后，让我们快速了解一下实施可靠的 DevSecOps 的主要好处

通过在开发过程的每个阶段，特别是在早期阶段考虑和实施安全性，组织正在主动减轻网络威胁。 这与自动化安全测试相结合意味着开发团队可以在不减慢开发周期的情况下执行安全检查和审计。

这增加了创新并导致更好的跨团队支持。 它还确保每个人都对安全负责。

在开发过程的每个阶段都具有安全性，这限制了以后发现漏洞的机会，这些漏洞可能会延迟发布。 作为开发过程的一部分，通过自动化安全测试来简化流程的过程还可以确保在每个阶段都发现可能的漏洞。

成本也始终是一个主要优势，通过建立良好的 DevSecOps 流程，您将更快地发现错误和问题，从而减少修复它们所需的时间和成本。



原文:

What is DevOps

Let's first take a look at what DevOps (Developer Operations) is so we can better understand why it has now evolved into DevSecOps (Developer Security Operations). DevOps is a combination of philosophies, practices, and tools that increases a business's ability to deliver better development in less time (Higher velocity). This can be applied to building a new product or the process of continuous improvement that applies to most products we see today.

If we look at the old way of development, typical teams worked on their own and would do their bit and then just pass it on to the next team to do theirs, a bit like an assembly line. This meant that development cycles were very long, communication between teams was limited, handover processes were not in place and if anything went wrong it would take a long time to identify and fix issues. If we compare that to today's agile development process we use DevOps to work in sprints and deliver results in a much faster and more efficient way. This has been made possible by not only the way we look at the development cycle but also by tools and automation.

DevOps has come a long way already and we have seen it evolve with the agile methodology of working but security, in particular, was always still the last step of the development process and not thought about as a necessity. In some cases when security was only looked at by a specific team at the end of the process.

Why has security been integrated into DevOps

The rise in cybercrime and the sophistication of attacks has made security a crucial part of the development process and not just an afterthought. This has led to security being integrated into the development process at each stage and becoming a shared responsibility. It has also led to security being thought about and planned right from the start of each process rather than at the end. This has resulted in more development velocity, more layers of integrated security and higher collaboration around security as a major aspect of the development cycle.

The integration of security in the development cycle at each stage from start to finish while also automating some of the security steps is what has led to the evolution of DevOps becoming DevSecOps, a go-to practice today. One of the main reasons the term was adjusted to include security is to make each person in the process think about and take accountability for secure building practices.

Benefits of DevSecOps

Lastly, let's take a quick look at the main benefits of having solid DevSecOps in place

1. Security

By thinking about and implementing security at each stage of the development process and particularly in the early stages organizations are proactively mitigating cyber threats. This combined with automated security testing means that dev teams can perform security checks and audits without slowing down development cycles.

2. Improved Collaboration

DevSecOps pushes for development and security and operations teams to collaborate more and especially in the early stages of the process. This increases innovation and leads to better cross-team buy-in. It also makes sure that everyone is responsible for security.

3. Streamlines process that limits vulnerabilities

Having security in every stage of the development process limits the chance of finding vulnerabilities much later, vulnerabilities which could delay the release. The process of streamlining the process with automated security testing as part of the development process also ensures that possible vulnerabilities are caught at each stage.

4. Cost

Cost is also always a major benefit and by having a good DevSecOps process in place you will find bugs and issues much faster which will result in less time and cost required to fix them.
Items.Author: jaysingh

Items.Title: 计算字长（映射方法） | Counting word lengths (map approach)
Items.Description:
在上一篇文章中，我们已经看到了如何使用数组来解决问题。

具体来说，我们有一个 12 长度的数组，每个位置用于根据当前单词的长度增加一个计数器。 我们必须通过从第一个数字中减去 1 来将字长转换为数组索引。

这个解决方案工作得非常好，并且考虑到只考虑最多 12 个字长的限制，我会走这条路。

但值得研究基于地图的方法（在其他语言中，“地图”被称为“字典”）。 使用这种方法可以更好地解决其他一些问题。

回顾

完整的基于数组的解决方案如下所示：

 import "listUtil" words = file.readLines("/sys/data/englishWords.txt") word_length_counters = [0] * 12 for word in words word_length = len(word) if word_length > 12 then continue counter_index = word_length - 1 current_count = word_length_counters[counter_index] word_length_counters[counter_index] = current_count + 1 end for max_count = word_length_counters.max max_count_index = word_length_counters.indexOf(max_count) max_word_length = max_count_index + 1 print "The most common word length is " + max_word_length

事实上，大部分代码可以保持不变。 但是需要做出一些关键的改变。

最初的变化

首先，我们将使用映射而不是将计数器存储在数组中。 这将是一个通用的空地图，如下所示：

 word_length_counters = {}

使用地图的一个优点是我们可以使用“人类”数字作为键。 也就是说：我们可以直接使用单词长度（从 1 到 12），而不必像数组索引那样减去 1。

特别是 MiniScript 允许您使用几乎任何东西作为地图索引，这非常方便。 它甚至允许您使用数字作为键 - 我们将在这里做。

但随后它变得有趣。 我们不需要这个：

 counter_index = word_length - 1

如前所述，我们可以通过word_length访问current_count ：

 current_count = word_length_counters[word_length]

并将其存储回来，同样使用word_length作为地图的键：

 word_length_counters[counter_index] = current_count + 1

发现问题

把它们放在一起，这就是 for 循环的样子：

 for word in words word_length = len(word) if word_length > 12 then continue current_count = word_length_counters[word_length] word_length_counters[word_length] = current_count + 1 end for

看起来不错，对吧？ 但是当你运行它时会发生什么？

 Runtime Error: Key Not Found: '1' not found in map [line 11]

这是为什么？ 问题是，地图最初是空的！ 因此，第一次要检索“current_count”时，什么也找不到。 我们需要处理地图还没有针对给定字长的计数器的情况。

我们需要问：这张地图是否有关键字“word_length”？ 如果没有，则为其添加一个 0 条目。这将具有在下一行中检索“current_count”将找到一些要添加 1 的内容（0 值）的效果。

首次访问

我们如何询问地图是否有某个“键”？ 在 MiniScript 中，这是在“集合”级别解决的。 那就是：有一种方法被称为相同的，并且对字符串、列表和映射的作用相似。 该方法是hasIndex 。

考虑到这一点，这是我们需要的行：

 if not word_length_counters.hasIndex(word_length) then word_length_counters[word_length] = 0

把它放在第一次访问之前会给我们这个（固定的）for循环：

 for word in words word_length = len(word) if word_length > 12 then continue if not word_length_counters.hasIndex(word_length) then word_length_counters[word_length] = 0 current_count = word_length_counters[word_length] word_length_counters[word_length] = current_count + 1 end for

结果计算

现在是调整结果计算的时候了。

在基于数组的方法word_length_counters已经包含了字长。 但是因为我们使用的是映射（从字长映射到字数），所以我们需要查看映射的值。

因此，第一行将从：

 max_count = word_length_counters.max

至：

 max_count = word_length_counters.values.max

因为 map 的键已经是 word-length，所以我们不需要从 "index" 转换为 "word-length"。 即使 MiniScript 是在谈论“索引”，它在地图的上下文中也确实意味着“键”。

因此，下一行可以简化为：

 max_count_index = word_length_counters.indexOf(max_count) max_word_length = max_count_index + 1

至：

 max_word_length = word_length_counters.indexOf(max_count)

最后的print保持不变。

完整的解决方案

最后，完整的基于地图的解决方案如下所示：

 import "listUtil" words = file.readLines("/sys/data/englishWords.txt") word_length_counters = {} for word in words word_length = len(word) if word_length > 12 then continue if not word_length_counters.hasIndex(word_length) then word_length_counters[word_length] = 0 current_count = word_length_counters[word_length] word_length_counters[word_length] = current_count + 1 end for max_count = word_length_counters.values.max max_word_length = word_length_counters.indexOf(max_count) print "The most common word length is " + max_word_length

希望一般情况下如何使用地图很清楚，尤其是在这种情况下（有趣的是，使用数字作为键）。



原文:

In a previous post we saw already how to solve the problem by using an array.

Specifically we had a 12-length array, and each position was used to increase a counter according to the current word's length. We had to convert from word-length to array-index by subtracting 1 from the first number.

This solution worked perfectly fine and given the constraint of only considering word-lengths up to 12, I would go this route.

But it is worth looking at a map-based approach (in other languages "map" is known as "dictionary"). Some other problems are better solved with this approach.

Recap

The complete array-based solution looked like this:

import "listUtil"

words = file.readLines("/sys/data/englishWords.txt")

word_length_counters = [0] * 12

for word in words
    word_length = len(word)
    if word_length > 12 then continue
    counter_index = word_length - 1
    current_count = word_length_counters[counter_index]
    word_length_counters[counter_index] = current_count + 1
end for

max_count = word_length_counters.max
max_count_index = word_length_counters.indexOf(max_count)
max_word_length = max_count_index + 1

print "The most common word length is " + max_word_length

Indeed, most of the code can remain the same. But some key changes need to be made.

First changes

First, instead of storing the counters in an array we will use a map. It will be a generic empty map, like this:

word_length_counters = {}

One advantage of using maps is that we can use "human" numbers as keys. That is: we can use directly the word-lenghts (which go from 1 to 12) without having to subtract 1 as we did for array indexes.

In particular MiniScript allows you to use pretty much anything as map-indexes, which is very convenient. It even allows you to use numbers as keys - which we will be doing here.

The first part of the for-loop remains the same:

for word in words
    word_length = len(word)
    if word_length > 12 then continue

But then it gets interesting. We don't need this:

counter_index = word_length - 1

We can access the current_count by word_length as mentioned:

current_count = word_length_counters[word_length]

And store it back, also using word_length as the key of the map:

word_length_counters[counter_index] = current_count + 1

Problem found

Putting it all together, this is how the for-loop would look like:

for word in words
    word_length = len(word)
    if word_length > 12 then continue
    current_count = word_length_counters[word_length]
    word_length_counters[word_length] = current_count + 1
end for

Looks ok, right? But what happens when you run it?

Runtime Error: Key Not Found: '1' not found in map [line 11]

Why is that? Problem is, the map is initially empty! So, the first time the "current_count" wants to be retrieved, nothing is found. We need to handle the case where the map does not yet have a counter for a given word-length.

We need to ask: does this map have the key "word_length"? If not, add an entry for it with a 0. This will have the effect that retrieving the "current_count" in the next line will find something (a 0 value) to add 1 to.

First-time access

How do we ask if a map has a certain "key"? In MiniScript this is solved at the "collection" level. That is: there is a method that is called the same and acts similarly for strings, lists and maps. That method is hasIndex.

With that in mind, this is the line we need:

if not word_length_counters.hasIndex(word_length) then word_length_counters[word_length] = 0

Putting that before the first access gives us then this (fixed) for-loop:

for word in words
    word_length = len(word)
    if word_length > 12 then continue
    if not word_length_counters.hasIndex(word_length) then word_length_counters[word_length] = 0
    current_count = word_length_counters[word_length]
    word_length_counters[word_length] = current_count + 1
end for

The result-calculation

Now is the time to adapt the result-calculation.

In the array-based approach word_length_counters already contained the word-lengths. But because we are using a map (which maps from word-length to word-counts) we need to look at the values of our map.

The first line would thus change from:

max_count = word_length_counters.max

to:

max_count = word_length_counters.values.max

Because the keys of the map are already word-lengths, we don't need to convert from "index" to "word-length". Even if MiniScript is talking about "index", it really means "key" in the context of a map.

Therefor the next lines can be simplified from:

max_count_index = word_length_counters.indexOf(max_count)
max_word_length = max_count_index + 1

to:

max_word_length = word_length_counters.indexOf(max_count)

The final print remains the same.

Complete solution

Finally, the complete map-based solution looks like this:

import "listUtil"

words = file.readLines("/sys/data/englishWords.txt")

word_length_counters = {}

for word in words
    word_length = len(word)
    if word_length > 12 then continue
    if not word_length_counters.hasIndex(word_length) then word_length_counters[word_length] = 0
    current_count = word_length_counters[word_length]
    word_length_counters[word_length] = current_count + 1
end for

max_count = word_length_counters.values.max
max_word_length = word_length_counters.indexOf(max_count)

print "The most common word length is " + max_word_length

Hopefully it was clear how to use maps in general, and in particular in this case (interestingly, using numbers as keys).

Items.Title: #SeniaBreaksTheInternet：我的科技之旅 | #SeniaBreaksTheInternet: My Journey Into Tech
Items.Description:
两个月前，我从未想过我会在这里写下我的科技之旅。

两个月前，我不是第一次考虑进入科技行业。 然而，45天前是我第一次看到机会并决定抓住它。 一旦我抓住机会，我就想“废话。 我让自己陷入了什么境地？”

我会解释为什么我一直犹豫不决，我为什么开始以及它是如何进行的。

全职喧嚣

我是一位拉丁 27 岁的第一次妈妈，在一家科技保险公司 Asurion 工作，她在有空的时候也追求对美丽的热情（如果你愿意的话，也是一种副业）。

我在项目管理/公司治理方面有一份全职工作（周一至周五，朝九晚五）。 下班后，我回到一个三口之家，主要是和我一岁的儿子一起享受时光，欣赏他是如何发现世界的。 在某些周末，我会抽出一点时间化妆来减压。

我的猜测是，你可能已经想知道我在哪里找到时间来做这一切，简短的回答是“我不知道”，但我要感谢我的伴侣和父母让我保持清醒。

这一切是如何开始的

这是在纳什维尔市中心办公室的另一个常规日子，在我们的一个沟通渠道中，我遇到了一篇关于内部开发者宣传计划的帖子。

一旦我读到它是关于什么的，我想哇，这些都是我感兴趣的东西，但我不是开发人员。 在一位 UX 设计师的好朋友的推动下，我决定申请这个项目。 我想我要么不明白，不需要做额外的工作，要么我明白了，至少会学到一件事。

我与负责该计划的开发者关系高级经理@bradstondev进行了面谈，我对我们的谈话进行的方式感到惊讶。 我参加会议时以为我会被拒绝，并告诉我至少需要有一些经验（顺便说一句，冒名顶替综合症是真实的）。 但与我的看法相反，我与经理的谈话让我大开眼界。

他毫不害羞地设定了我必须投入多少额外工作才能了解这个新世界的期望。 我立即认为这是不可能的，因为我的很多时间已经被其他事情占用了。 我开始思考并想出“如果不是现在，那么什么时候？”。 然后我考虑了所有事情并决定去做，因为与以前类似，在提交我的申请时，我的选择是要么尝试学习一些东西，要么成功并学到比我想象的更多的东西。

我这样做的原因

在权衡了我的选择之后，我得出了为什么我真的想首先进入科技行业； 我想打破障碍，成为一个有影响力的人，并成为当你想做一些看似无法实现的事情时会发生什么的证据。 我想进入科技行业已经有一段时间了，但总是对从哪里开始以及如何开始感到害怕。 我还没有遇到很多看起来像我或面临相同挑战的技术人员。

自然地，我开始进行研究，发现只有 27% 的女性在科技行业担任这些职位，其中只有 2% 是西班牙裔或拉丁裔的女性。

这些数字让我感到难过，也更加激励了我。

它激励我希望激励其他少数族裔停止被低估，并通过分享对我有用的东西和不适合我的东西，致力于成为这个世界需要的解决方案的一部分。 我想证明，如果我能做到，那么你肯定也能做到，无论你是父母、移民还是认为为时已晚的人。

永远不会太晚，因为有了意识，您已经成为平等变革的一部分。 有很多技术是在没有考虑儿童、少数族裔或女性的情况下开发的。

你知道安全带最初是为男性设计的吗？

作为一个女人，很明显情况可能是这样的，因为安全带在你的胸部或宝宝的肚子上感觉很不舒服。

你试过在飞机上给孩子换尿布吗？

如果你有，那么你就会知道没有专门的空间来放置你的宝宝、放置你的尿布袋，或者有足够的空间来舒适地让父母和他们的宝宝的过程更容易。

这就是他们需要我以及他们需要你的原因。

但是从哪里开始呢？

事情是这样的，我没有编程经验。 我最后一次编写代码是在 2011 年更新我的 Tumblr 个人资料，而我第一次编写代码是在 2006 年更改 MySpace 上的布局。 我什至不记得我做了什么，也不记得我是怎么发现的！ 这个想法激发了，如果我能在 12 岁和 14 岁时做到这一点，我当然可以在 20 多岁时做到。

所以我决定开始看一些 TikToks、Youtube 上的视频，甚至还跟真正的开发者见了面。 当我第一次有机会在工作中跟随软件工程师时，感觉就像在看代码时，我正在阅读象形文字。 当我说看起来有人打算开始输入一个句子但决定在句子中间敲击键盘并称之为“编码”时，我并没有夸大其词。

我很震惊外面的人知道如何做到这一点并享受它。 我迷失了方向，我又一次灰心丧气，心想：“我到底怎么会明白这一点，为什么这对我在七年级的时候有意义？” 即使听到人们谈论代码似乎也是一种不同的语言。

我非常想把这些点连接起来，但感觉就像凝视着一个繁星点点的夜晚，被告知要看一个特定的星座，但你只是看不到它。

嗯，这都是关于观点和你如何学习的。

我会告诉你，当我想学习如何在你正在阅读的这个博客上添加 gif 时，我第一次知道它并没有那么糟糕。 您正在阅读的博客是在一个面向开发人员的平台上，我不是开发人员，所以我不能像在 Microsoft Word 中一样粘贴图像。 我不得不编码只是为了让这个词加粗。

老实说，在我寻求帮助之前，我已经厌倦了这个烂摊子。 突然间，我开始看到我的第一个星座。 如果你愿意的话，北斗七星。 尝试了几次，我不得不继续回去测试，看看我是否正确地添加了模式和 URL，但是当我看到 gif 工作的那一刻，感觉就像我解决了世界上最难的数学问题。

我很确定这就是凯瑟琳·约翰逊（Katherine Johnson）在完成她的方程式让人类最终登上月球后的感受。

我试图理解代码的实际表现。

下一章的开始

这只是我学习技术的故事的开始。

说实话，我现在的梦想不是成为一名软件工程师。 我的目标是看看我能不能做到，最重要的是，我是否喜欢它。 我很有可能被错误的事情所激励，最终讨厌整个过程，或者更糟糕的是，我可能认为我正在创建一个网站，最终侵入 FBI 总部，破坏互联网，并找到一种方法来原谅每个人的助学贷款。

哦等等，也许我不只是添加一个 gif。

不管这把我带到哪里，我知道这正是我应该去的地方，我会尽力而为。 我的看法是，我要么找到对编程的隐藏热情，要么找出更适合我的科技领域。 我很享受学习网络的基本功能，希望你离开时也想了解更多关于如何开始的信息。

为打破互联网干杯……从字面上看。



原文:

Two months ago I would’ve never imagined I’d be here writing about my journey into tech.

Two months ago was not the first time I thought about getting into tech. However, 45 days ago was the first time I saw an opportunity and decided to take it. Once I took the opportunity I thought “Crap. What did I get myself into?”

I’ll explain why I’ve been hesitant, why I started and how it’s going.

Full-time Hustle

I’m a Latin 27-year-old first-time mom who works for a tech insurance company, Asurion, who also pursues a passion in beauty when available (a side hustle, if you will).

I have a full-time job in project management/corporate governance (Monday-Friday, 9 to 5). After work, I come home to a family of 3 to mainly enjoy time with my one-year-old son and admire how he discovers the world. and on some weekends, I make a little time to decompress by doing makeup.

My guess, is that you’re probably already wondering where I find the time for all of this and the short answer is "I don’t know", but I have my partner and our parents to thank for keeping me sane.

How it all Began

It's another regular day in the office in downtown Nashville when in one of our communication channels I ran into a post about an internal Developer Advocacy Program.

Once I read what it was about I thought wow these are all things I’m interested in but I am not a developer. With the push of a good friend who is a UX Designer, I decided to apply for the program. I figured I either don’t get it and don’t have to do extra work or I get it and will learn at least one thing.

I had my interview with the Developer Relations Senior Manager, @bradstondev, who was leading the program and I was surprised at how our conversation carried. I went into the meeting thinking I’ll be turned down and told I need to have at least SOME experience (imposter syndrome is real by the way). But contrary to my belief, my conversation with the manager lead to a great awakening of inspiration.

He was not shy to set expectations of how much extra work I’d have to put in to have an understanding of this new world. I immediately thought it won’t be possible because so much of my time is already taken away by everything else. I got to thinking and figured "if not now then when?". I then considered everything and decided to go for it because similar to before, when submitting my application, my options were either I try and learn something or succeed and learn more than I imagined.

The Reason Why I Do It

After weighing out my options, I concluded why I actually wanted to get into tech in the first place; I want to break barriers, be an influencer, and be evidence of what can happen when you want to do something that seems unattainable. I’ve wanted to get into tech for some time but was always intimidated on where and how to start. I haven’t run into many people in tech that look like me or have shared the same challenges.

Naturally, I started doing research and found out that only 27% of women take up these positions in tech and only 2% of them are women who identify as Hispanic or Latino.

Those numbers saddened me and motivated me even more.

It motivated me to hopefully inspire other minorities to stop being underrepresented and aim to be part of the solution this world needs by sharing what works and what doesn’t for me. I want to prove that if I can do it, then you definitely can do it too, no matter if you are a parent, an immigrant, or the one thinking it's too late.

It’s never too late because with awareness you are already part of the change to equality. There’s so much technology out there that was developed without the consideration of children, minorities, or women.

Did you know that seatbelts were originally designed for men?

As a woman, It's very apparent that that might be the case because of how uncomfortable seatbelts feel over your chest or your baby bump.

Have you tried changing a child’s diaper on the plane?

If you have then you’d know there’s no dedicated space to lay your baby, set your diaper bag, or have enough room to comfortably make the process easier for a parent and their baby.

This is why they need me and why they need YOU.

But Where to Start?

Here's the thing, I have no experience in programming. The last time I did coding was in 2011 updating my Tumblr profile and the first time I did coding was in 2006 changing the layout on MySpace. I don’t even remember what I did or how I even found out! The thought sparked up, if I can do it at 12 and 14 years old I certainly can do it in my 20s.

So I decided to start watching a few TikToks, videos on Youtube, and even shadowed a real developer. While having the opportunity to shadow a software engineer at work for the first time, it felt like, while looking at the code, I was reading hieroglyphics. I am not exaggerating when I say it looked like someone meant to start typing a sentence but decided mid-sentence to slam the keyboard across their knee and call it “coding”.

I was shocked that people out there know how to do this and enjoy it. I was so lost and I once again was discouraged and thought to myself, "How in the hell will I ever understand this and why did this make sense to me in 7th grade?" Even hearing people talk about code seems like a different language.

I wanted to connect the dots so badly but instead it felt like staring into a starry night and being told to look at a specific constellation but you just don’t see it.

Well, it's all about perspective and how you learn.

I’ll tell you that the first time I knew that it wasn’t that bad was when I wanted to learn how to add a gif to this very blog you are reading. The blog you’re reading is on a platform for developers and I’M NOT A DEVELOPER, so it’s not like I can just paste the image as if I’m in Microsoft Word. I had to code just to make this word bold.

To be honest, I was already I was fed up with this mess until I asked for help. All of a sudden I started to see my first constellation. The big dipper if you will. It took a few tries and I had to keep going back and testing to see if I was adding the patterns and URLs correctly, but the second I saw that gif working, it felt like I had solved the world’s hardest math problem.

I’m pretty sure this is how Katherine Johnson felt once she finished her equation to get man finally on the moon.

Actual representation of me trying to understand code.

Beginning of the Next Chapter

This is just the beginning of my story of how I’m learning tech.

Truth be told, my dream as of now is not to become a software engineer. My goal is to see if I can do it and, most importantly, if I like it. There’s a good chance I may be motivated by the wrong things and end up hating the whole process or even worse I could think I’m creating a website and end up hacking into FBI headquarters, breaking the internet, and finding a way to forgive everyone’s student loans.

Oh wait, maybe I wasn’t only adding a gif.

Regardless of where this takes me, I know it's exactly where I’m supposed to be and I’m going to make the best of it. The way I see it is that I’ll either find a hidden passion for programming or I’ll find out what else is out there in tech that is a better suit for me. I’m enjoying learning the basic functions of the web and hope you leave wanting to know more about how you can get started too.

Cheers to breaking the internet... literally.

Items.Title: 第644天：天堂 | Day 644 : Paradise
Items.Description:
嘻哈和代码

发表于班轮注意事项：

  • 专业人士：举行了几次会议，致力于让我对博客文章所做的更新获得批准。 还修复了检查我正在制作的一些 Web 组件的演示应用程序的方法。 不是糟糕的一天。

  • 个人：昨晚我玩了 Meta Quest Pro。 它太酷了。 我可以在我的网站上玩带有音频聊天的 WebXR Tic Tac Toe 游戏，但我还不知道如何打开相机。 我做了更多的研究，发现了一个视频，它看起来像是一个人访问了一个网站，并且能够让相机激活并进入增强现实。 我还观看了我在最近的旅行中录制的一些 360 度视频。 站在中间很酷，可以环顾四周，重温这些时刻。 让我想购买更高分辨率的更新的 360 相机。 另外，我把你戴上耳机时进入的房间设置为日本的老校舍。 池塘里的锦鲤鱼很平静。 很像天堂。 观看了新的“电锯人”剧集并赶上了“Mob Psycho 100”。

打算再玩一下 Meta Quest Pro。 主要看我是否可以访问允许直通摄像头打开的站点。 我想在天黑之前完成它，因为我昨晚正在这样做，而通过并不是那么好。 在那之后，我想在我一直在做的副项目的前端工作。 我想到了一个我想尝试的布局。 可能会看一些动漫。 我认为可能会有一个新的“漂白剂”出来。

有一个美好的夜晚！

HIPHOP and CODE

Posted on Oct 26

liner notes:

  • Professional : Had a few meetings, worked on getting the updates I made to a blog post approved. Also fixed the way to check out a demo application for some Web Components I'm making. Not a bad day.

  • Personal : Last night I played around with the Meta Quest Pro. It's pretty cool. I was able to play a game of WebXR Tic Tac Toe with audio chat on my site, but I couldn't figure out how to turn on the camera yet. I did some more research and found a video where it looks like a person went to a website and was able to get the camera to activate and go into augmented reality. Have a great night!

Items.Title: 使用独立 API（没有 NgModule）手动延迟加载 Angular 组件和提供程序🥢 | Manual lazy loading of Angular component and providers with Standalone APIs (without NgModule) 🥢
Items.Description:
克日什托夫·普拉蒂斯

发表于使用Angular Standalone API （在 v14 中引入）可以手动延迟加载组件（即使使用它的依赖服务和提供程序），类似于我们手动延迟加载NgModule的方式。 我们只需要为自己创建一个子EnvironmentInjector （模拟延迟加载的NgModule会做什么）。 这也正是 Angular Router自 v14 以来所做的，当为具有自己的providers数组的Route实例化组件时。

例如，假设我们有一个桶index.ts ，其中包含我们想要延迟加载的项目 - 一个组件和一个服务。 让我们假设组件依赖于服务。

 // lazy/index.ts export * from ' ./lazy.service ' ; export * from ' ./lazy.component ' ; // custom naming convention - export an array named `providers`: export const providers = [ LazyService ];

然后我们可以延迟加载这个桶并创建一个子EnvironmentInjector （包含所有桶的providers ）。 稍后可以在实例化惰性组件时使用此注入器（因此该组件可以访问上述providers ）。

 export class AppComponent { constructor ( protected viewContainerRef : ViewContainerRef , protected injector : Injector , protected environmentInjector : EnvironmentInjector ) {} lazyLoadComponent () { // 1. lazy load the barrel file import ( ' ./lazy/index ' ). then (( lazyBarrel ) => { // 2. create manually an `EnvironmentInjector` with all // the `providers` exported from the lazy barrel. // Pass `this.environmentInjector` as a parent. const childEnvironmentInjector = createEnvironmentInjector ( lazyBarrel . providers , this . environmentInjector , ' Lazy Environment Injector ' ); // 3. instantiate a lazy component, passing: // the parent component's element `Injector` // and the just created child `EnvironmentInjector` const lazyComponent = createComponent ( lazyBarrel . LazyComponent , { environmentInjector : childEnvironmentInjector , elementInjector : this . injector , }); // 4. attach the lazy component inside the parent component this . viewContainerRef . insert ( lazyComponent . hostView ); }); }

路由驱动的懒加载——Angular 14源码分析

从版本 14 开始，上述方法与 Angular Router在底层实例化组件（不仅是延迟加载）的方式非常相似。 当将 URL 与包含providers数组的Route匹配时，Angular 会创建一个子EnvironmentInjector 。 稍后，当<router-outlet>为当前Route实例化一个组件时，Angular 会采用Route的EnvironmentInjector （或在父Routes中定义的最近的注入器） ，然后在创建组件实例时使用这个EnvironmentInjector ，所以组件可以访问Route的providers 。

原文:

Krzysztof Platis

Posted on Oct 26

With Angular Standalone APIs (introduced in v14) it’s possible to manually lazy load a component (even with it’s dependency services and providers), similarly to how we would manually lazy load a NgModule. We just need to create ourselves a child EnvironmentInjector (simulating what a lazy-loaded NgModule would do). This is also exactly what the Angular Router does since v14, when instantiating a component for a Route that has it's own providers array.

TLDR: See the stackblitz example of manually lazy loading a component with a service, with Standalone APIs.

For example, let's say we have a barrel index.ts with items that we want to lazy load all together - a component and a service. And let's suppose the component depends on the service.

// lazy/index.ts export * from './lazy.service';
export * from './lazy.component'; // custom naming convention - export an array named `providers`:
export const providers = [LazyService];

Then we can lazy load this barrel and create a child EnvironmentInjector (containing all barrel's providers). Later on this injector can be used when instantiating the lazy component (so the component has the access to the providers mentioned above).

export class AppComponent { constructor( protected viewContainerRef: ViewContainerRef, protected injector: Injector, protected environmentInjector: EnvironmentInjector ) {} lazyLoadComponent() { // 1. lazy load the barrel file import('./lazy/index').then((lazyBarrel) => { // 2. create manually an `EnvironmentInjector` with all // the `providers` exported from the lazy barrel. // Pass `this.environmentInjector` as a parent. const childEnvironmentInjector = createEnvironmentInjector( lazyBarrel.providers, this.environmentInjector, 'Lazy Environment Injector' ); // 3. instantiate a lazy component, passing: // the parent component's element `Injector` // and the just created child `EnvironmentInjector` const lazyComponent = createComponent(lazyBarrel.LazyComponent, { environmentInjector: childEnvironmentInjector, elementInjector: this.injector, }); // 4. attach the lazy component inside the parent component this.viewContainerRef.insert(lazyComponent.hostView); }); }

Routing-driven lazy loading - Angular 14 source code analysis

The above approach is very similar to how the Angular Router instantiates components (not only lazy-loaded) under the hood, since version 14. When matching an URL against a Route that contains an array of providers, Angular creates a child EnvironmentInjector. Later on, when the <router-outlet> instantiates a component for the current Route, Angular takes the Route's EnvironmentInjector (or the closest injector defined in the parent Routes) and then it uses this EnvironmentInjector when creating a component instance, so the component has access to the Route's providers.

Items.Title: 我的第一个 AWS Amplify 应用程序背后的故事 | The story behind my first AWS Amplify App
Items.Description:
自 2016 年左右以来，我一直是制作生产应用程序的独立开发人员。这些应用程序中的大多数只是用于了解更多信息的应用程序，但投入生产是为了让人们可以在我学习的过程中使用我正在构建的东西。 这些产品中的大多数已不再生产并且已被弃用，因为它们要花钱，而且没有收入来源可以让它们回来。

这导致了一些离线电话，在快速验证概念应用程序后，我发布了Shadow Flips的第一个版本。 我利用过去 3 年一直在学习和构建的东西，在 Google Firebase 上构建了一个无服务器的云托管后端，一个使用Ionic 框架的前端 Web 应用程序，托管在Twitch 的 Extension Store上，以及一个独立的桌面带有 Ionic Framework 的应用程序，但在Electron应用程序中运行。 我之所以选择这些工具和框架，是因为我在大学学习了 Ionic 和 Firebase，在那里我参加了移动开发课程。 多年来，我学会了如何保持我的云成本极低和非常优化。

我通过 Twitch 的人脉被介绍给 AWS Amplify，并被邀请成为Amplify Discord Server的服务器管理员之一。 然后我开始了解 Amplify 及其所有功能，当我开始研究 AWS Amplify 以取代 firebase 时，由于 AWS 在定价方面的耻辱，这非常令人生畏。 在 Shadow Flips 中，我能够将每月的成本保持在 1 美元以下，因此迁移到 AWS 对我的钱包来说真是个打击，但我喜欢 AWS Amplify 背后的想法，我想给它一个去。

今年，我的工作团队决定为我们的团队构建一个内部应用程序来帮助自动化任务，并构建一个仪表板来帮助我们的工作。 我认为这是我使用 AWS Amplify 的机会。 我写了一个演示文档，介绍了为什么我们应该使用 AWS Amplify 而不是自己启动所有后端资源，以及为什么我们应该使用 Ionic Framework 来构建应用程序。 我的症结在于我们可以多快地迭代代码和启动后端，而无需使用Amplify CLI事先了解 AWS。 我们的团队不是 SDE，但我们有计算机科学知识，但没有太多实践经验。 我是我们团队中最有经验的，但只是作为一名独立开发者。 我们确实有一个由 SDE 组成的姊妹团队，但这是我们要构建的项目。

我们启动了 Amplify 后端，构建了一个基本的前端，然后开始着手。 因为这是一个内部应用程序，所以我必须开发的第一件事就是将我们所有的工作锁定在公司 SSO 之后。 这似乎也很令人生畏，但是 Amplify 文档、Amplify Discord、Stack Overflow 等在线提供的资源确实使这项任务比我最初预期的要容易得多。 仍然必须进入 Cognito 并设置联合身份的学习曲线仍然比 Firebase 复杂得多以启动自定义身份验证，但最后，我不必编写任何代码来让它工作，这是一个Firebase 的巨大优势，当我想将非标准身份集成到我的应用程序中时，我必须编写自定义云功能。

我们开始在日常活动中使用该应用程序，它帮助我们在门票上返回 TTR。 使用了几个月，我认为使用范围与我在 Firebase 上开发的其他应用程序非常相似，包括 Shadow Flips，并且比较成本，AWS Amplify 略高，但仍在运行每月只有几美元。 因此，任何来自您的应用程序的小额资金或任何小额收入流，虽然它仍然很小，但在 AWS Amplify 上非常易于管理，因为这是我担心的事情，首先是我不想切换的原因. 登录您的 DEV 帐户以获得更精心策划的体验。

原文:

I have been an indie developer making production apps since around 2016. Most of these apps were just apps to learn more, but were put into production to people could use what I was building as I was learning. Most of these products are no longer in production and have been deprecated, as they cost money, and have no revenue streams to make them back.

In 2019, I was watching a streamer BruisedLee01 on Twitch.tv, and he was joking around saying that someone should buy his bot and make it a public feature on Twitch, and then I make a joke to a streamer on Twitch.tv about just doing it ourselves and releasing it as a Twitch Extension for public consumption. This lead to a few offline calls and after a quick proof of concept app, I released the first version of Shadow Flips. I took what I have been learning and building for the previous 3 years, and build a serverless cloud-hosted backend on Google Firebase, a front-end web app using Ionic Framework hosted on Twitch's Extension Store, as well as a stand-alone desktop app with Ionic Framework as well, but running in an Electron app. I chose these tools and frameworks because I was taught Ionic and Firebase at my university where I took a mobile development course. Over the years, I learned how to keep my cloud costs extremely low and very optimized.

I was introduced to AWS Amplify from my connections at Twitch and was invited to be one of the server admins of the Amplify Discord Server. I then started to learn about Amplify and all of its features, and when I started to look into AWS Amplify to replace firebase, it was very daunting because of the stigma that AWS has around pricing. In Shadow Flips, I was able to keep my monthly costs well under $1 a month, so moving over to AWS would have been a real shell shock to my wallet, but I loved the idea behind AWS Amplify, and I wanted to give it a go.

This year, my team at work decided to build an internal application for our team to help automate tasks, and build a dashboard to help with our work. I thought this is my opportunity to use AWS Amplify. I wrote up a presentation doc on why we should use AWS Amplify instead of spinning up all of the backend resources ourselves, and why we should use Ionic Framework to build the app. My sticking points were how quickly we can iterate on code and spin up backends without having to know AWS beforehand by using the Amplify CLI. Our team are not SDEs, but we have knowledge about Computer Science, but not much practical experience. I am the most experienced on our team, but only as an indie developer. We do have a sister team that is made of SDEs, but this was our project to build out.

We spun up the Amplify backend, built a rudimentary front end, and started going. The first thing I had to develop since this is an internal app, is locking all of our work behind our corporate SSO. This seemed to be pretty daunting as well, but the resources available online in the Amplify docs, Amplify Discord, Stack Overflow, etc. really make the task much easier than I originally expected. The learning curve on still having to go into Cognito and setup federated identities was still a lot more complex than Firebase to spin up custom authentication, but in the end, I didn't have to write any code to get it working, which is a huge benefit over Firebase, where I had to write custom cloud functions when I wanted to integrate non-standard identities into my apps.

We started to use the app in our day-to-day activities, and it has helped return our TTR on tickets. Having used it for a few months, I believe the scope of the usage, in my opinion, would be very similar to other apps I have developed on Firebase, including Shadow Flips, and comparing costs, AWS Amplify was slightly higher, but still running only a few dollars a month. So any small bankroll, or any small income streams from your app, while it is still small, is very manageable on AWS Amplify, as that was the thing I was worried about, to begin with on why I didn't want to switch over. AWS Amplify also has a free tier on AWS, so if you are just building your app and plan to have a revenue stream within a year, it's worthwhile to test out AWS Amplify to see if it will suit your needs.

Items.Title: 像黑客一样思考：私有存储库中的 AWS 密钥 | Thinking Like a Hacker: AWS Keys in Private Repos
Items.Description:
上次，我们介绍了一个场景，其中威胁行为者发现“Poor Corp.”的 TLS 通配符证书使用的泄露的 RSA 私钥。 通过将泄露的私钥与 DNS 错误配置链接起来，攻击者能够冒充可怜的公司子域并使用它来创建高效的网络钓鱼电子邮件。

在本系列中，我们不仅会剖析攻击者可以做些什么来获取凭据，还会剖析他们在获得初始访问权限后会做些什么。 我们将在本系列的每个部分中介绍不同的威胁场景，并讲述恶意黑客的故事，这些故事要么是真实的，基于真实事件，要么是合理的理论。

低估私有 git 存储库

这一次，我们正在探索发生在另一个“可怜的公司”身上的真实数据泄露事件。 这种攻击与许多其他违规行为一样开始：通过成功的网络钓鱼攻击。

在这种情况下，威胁参与者一直在大型活动中发送网络钓鱼电子邮件，并且他们已经开始积累大量凭据。 随着新凭据的传入，恶意黑客注意到他们成功地在大型知名公司Poor Corp 对某人进行了网络钓鱼。 黑客对他们成功进行网络钓鱼的员工进行了一些 OSINT（开源情报）收集，他们发现他们拥有 Poor Corp 软件工程师的证书。

黑客利用软件工程师的凭证做的第一件事是发现他们可以访问Poor Corp 的哪些服务。 在伪装成软件工程师的同时，恶意黑客发现他们被盗的凭据可以登录到 Poor Corp 的 GitHub 组织。 为了获得代码库的本地副本，黑客克隆了 Poor Corp 的所有私人存储库。 然后，他们在每个 repo 上使用像Gitleaks这样的开源工具，看看他们是否能找到任何有效的秘密。 不幸的是，对于可怜的公司来说，他们的软件工程师没有像他们的开源代码那样对他们的内部代码进行严格的审查，我们的黑客在一些私人存储库中发现了硬编码的 AWS 密钥。

借助Poor Corp 代码中的AWS 密钥，我们的黑客能够访问一项服务，该服务为Poor Corp 的主要SaaS（软件即服务）产品处理计算任务。 该服务内部是Poor Corp 客户信息的档案——而且数量也不小。 该服务包含Poor Corp 约700 万客户的个人数据。

有了这么多的个人信息，恶意黑客决定他们可以通过窃取数据并勒索他们来敲诈可怜的公司。 从 AWS 档案中复制所有数据后，黑客向Poor Corp 发送了一封电子邮件。该电子邮件称，除非Poor Corp 向黑客付款，否则他们客户的数据将被泄露。 对黑客来说幸运的是，Poor Corp 因不正当的商业行为而声名狼藉，他们决定以 100,000 美元的价格收买黑客的沉默。

可怜的公司掩盖了这一事件，黑客得以逃脱勒索。 直到几年后，该违规行为才最终被公开披露。 到那时，我们的黑客早已不复存在，所有证据都被抹去。

得到教训

从这种现实世界的数据泄露中得到的第一个结论是，能够抵御网络钓鱼攻击的控制措施的重要性。 网络钓鱼培训计划可以提供帮助，但更重要的是拥有可以限制成功网络钓鱼影响的技术控制。

在这种情况下，如果 Poor Corp 的软件工程师在他们的 GitHub 帐户上设置了 MFA（多重身份验证） ，那么黑客可能无法访问 Poor Corp 的私人 GitHub 存储库。 对不常见的登录属性（位置、设备等）发出警报也可能向Poor Corp 暗示他们的软件工程师的帐户已被接管。

网络钓鱼是任何数据泄露最常见的初始访问媒介，攻击者越难以接管您的帐户，您就能越早防止或检测到泄露。

这次攻击的第二个收获是，私人回购与公共回购一样重要。 但是，一旦他们的一名软件工程师被钓鱼，他们的存储库就会暴露给组织外部的恶意人员。

最后，这种情况凸显了 Poor Corp 在掩盖数据泄露方面的不当行为。 即使他们付钱让黑客不泄露客户的私人数据，Poor Corp 仍然不能保证这些数据最终不会被滥用或泄露，他们的客户应该受到警告。

当发生这样的数据泄露事件时，它永远不会很好地反映一家公司，但当公司试图掩盖它并让其客户更加脆弱时，情况会更糟。 幸运的是，政府组织正在采取行动改善数据泄露报告的状态。 然而，企业领导者最终要认识到披露网络攻击的重要性并采取行动。

人们很容易认为扫描面向公众的存储库中的秘密很重要，但这种现实世界的数据泄露证明，从安全角度来看，您需要对所有代码一视同仁。 恶意黑客可以使用Gitleaks和TruffleHog等开源工具快速检测大量代码中的秘密* ，而不会留下任何痕迹。 作为一名防御者，**将秘密扫描紧密集成到您的 SDLC *（软件开发生命周期）中以降低暴露它们的风险非常重要。 安全工程师应该使用它在其内部边界（私有存储库、CI 管道、Jira 票证等）中植入虚假的 AWS 机密，以便在黑客试图利用它们时立即检测到入侵。



原文:

Last time, we covered a scenario in which a threat actor found leaked RSA private keys used by a TLS wildcard certificate for “Poor Corp.” By chaining the leaked private key with DNS misconfigurations, the attacker was able to impersonate a Poor Corp subdomain and use it to create highly effective phishing emails.

In this series, we will dissect not just what an attacker can do to get access to credentials, but also what they would do after getting that initial access. We will walk through a different threat scenario in each part of the series and tell stories of malicious hackers that are either true, based on a true incident, or reasonably theoretical.

Underestimating private git repositories

This time, we are exploring a real-world data breach that happened to another “Poor Corp.” This attack begins like many other breaches: with a successful phishing attack.

The threat actor in this scenario has been sending out phishing emails in a large campaign, and they’ve started to rack up a lot of credentials. As new credentials are coming in, the malicious hacker notices that they successfully phished someone at Poor Corp, a large, well-known company. The hacker does some OSINT (Open-Source INTelligence) gathering on the employee that they successfully phished, and they discover that they have the credentials of a Poor Corp software engineer.

The first thing the hacker does with the software engineer’s credentials is discovering which of Poor Corp’s services they can access. While masquerading as the software engineer, the malicious hacker discovers that their stolen credentials can log into Poor Corp’s GitHub organization. Poor Corp has several open-source repositories, but our hacker is more interested in what they couldn’t access before: private repositories.

To get a local copy of the codebase, the hacker clones all of Poor Corp’s private repos. Then, they use an open-source tool like Gitleaks on each repo to see if they could find any valid secrets. Unfortunately for Poor Corp, their software engineers hadn’t given their internal code as much scrutiny as their open-source code, and our hacker found AWS keys hard-coded in a few private repos.

AWS data exfiltration and extortion

With the AWS keys that were in Poor Corp’s code, our hacker was able to access a service that handled computing tasks for Poor Corp’s major SaaS (Software as a Service) offering. Inside that service was an archive of Poor Corp’s customer information – and not a small amount, either. The service contained the personal data of about 7 million of Poor Corp’s customers.

With this much personal information, the malicious hacker decided they could extort Poor Corp by stealing the data and blackmailing them. After copying all the data from the AWS archive, the hacker sent an email to Poor Corp. The email said that their customers’ data would be leaked unless Poor Corp paid the hacker. Luckily for the hacker, Poor Corp had a bad reputation for shady business practices, and they decided to buy the hacker’s silence for $100,000.

Poor Corp covered up the incident, and the hacker was able to get away with the extortion. It wasn’t until years later that the breach was finally disclosed publicly. By that point, our hacker was long gone, and all evidence was erased.

Lessons learned

The first takeaway from this real-world data breach is the importance of controls that provide resistance to phishing attacks. Phishing training programs can help, but it’s even more important to have technical controls in place that can limit the impact of a successful phish.

In this scenario, if Poor Corp’s software engineer had MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) set up on their GitHub account, the hacker probably wouldn’t have been able to access Poor Corp’s private GitHub repos. Alerting on uncommon login properties (location, device, etc.) also could have tipped off Poor Corp that their software engineer’s account was taken over.

Phishing is the most common initial access vector for any data breach, and the harder you make it for attackers to take over your accounts, the sooner you can prevent or detect a breach.

The second takeaway from this attack is that private repos are just as important to secure as public repos. Poor Corp got lazy with their code hygiene in their private GitHub repos, and it was probably because they thought only their employees’ accounts could access them. But as soon as one of their software engineers was phished, that exposed their repos to someone outside of their organization with bad intentions.

Lastly, this scenario highlights the misconduct of Poor Corp in covering up the data breach. Even though they paid off the hacker to not leak the private data of their customers, Poor Corp still had no guarantees that the data wouldn’t be misused or leaked eventually, and their customers deserved to be warned.

It never reflects well on a company when a data breach like this happens, but it’s even worse when the company tries to cover it up and leaves its customers even more vulnerable. Luckily, government organizations are taking action to improve the state of data breach reporting. However, it ultimately falls on business leaders to recognize the importance of disclosing cyber-attacks and take action.

How can Poor Corp enhance its security posture?

It’s easy to think that it’s only important to scan for secrets in your public-facing repositories, but this real-world data breach proves that you need to treat all code the same from a security perspective. Malicious hackers can use open-source tools like Gitleaks and TruffleHog to quickly detect secrets in massive amounts of code*, without leaving a trace. As a defender, **it’s extremely important to have secret scans tightly integrated into your SDLC* (software development lifecycle) to reduce the risks of exposing them. GitGuardian offers secret scanning for private repositories in their Free, Business, and Enterprise plans.

GitGuardian has also recently introduced an open-source tool that defenders can add to their arsenal, ggcanary – the GitGuardian Canary Tokens. Security engineers should use it to plant fake AWS secrets in their internal perimeter (private repositories, CI pipelines, Jira tickets, etc.) to detect intrusion as soon as hackers attempt to exploit them.

Items.Title: 活动记录验证 | Active Record Validations
Items.Description:
什么是验证？

这是一个例子。

 class User < ApplicationRecord validates :name, presence: true end
 irb> User.create(name: "John Doe").valid? => true irb> User.create(name: nil).valid? => false

验证让我们知道我们的用户没有名称属性是无效的。 第二个用户不会被持久化到数据库，因为它没有通过验证。

为什么使用验证？

验证用于确保仅将有效数据保存到数据库中。 例如，在您的应用程序中确保每个用户提供有效的用户名和密码可能很重要。 模型级验证是确保仅将有效数据保存到数据库中的最佳方式。 最终用户无法绕过验证，并且便于测试和维护。 在将数据保存到数据库之前，还有其他几种方法可以验证数据，包括本地数据库约束、客户端验证和控制器级验证。

何时进行验证？

有两种 Active Record 对象：一种对应于数据库中的一行，另一种不对应。 当您创建一个新对象时，例如使用new方法，该对象还不属于数据库。 一旦对该对象调用 save ，它将被保存到适当的数据库表中。 实例方法来确定对象是否已经在数据库中。 在这个例子中，我们有一个没有验证的 Active Record 类：

 irb> u = User.new(username: "Johnny") => #<User id: nil, username: "Johnny", created_at: nil, updated_at: nil> irb> u.new_record? => true irb> u.save => true irb> u.new_record? => false

创建和保存新记录将向数据库发送 SQL INSERT 操作。 更新现有记录将改为发送 SQL UPDATE 操作。 验证通常在这些命令发送到数据库之前运行。 如果任何验证失败，对象将被标记为无效，Active Record 将不会执行 INSERT 或 UPDATE 操作。 这可以防止我们在数据库中存储无效对象。 您可以选择在创建、保存或更新对象时运行特定验证。

以下方法触发验证，并且仅当对象有效时才会将对象保存到数据库中：

  • 创造

  • 创造！

  • 节省

  • 节省！

  • 更新

  • 更新！

如果记录无效，方法末尾的 bang 运算符 ("!") 会引发异常。 非爆炸版本不会：save 和 update 返回 false，create 返回对象。

以下方法跳过验证，并将对象保存到数据库中，无论它是否有效。 他们应该小心使用。

  • 减量！
  • 递减计数器
  • 增量！
  • 增量计数器
  • 插入
  • 插入！
  • 插入全部
  • 全部插入！
  • 切换！
  • 触碰
  • 全部更新
  • 更新属性
  • 更新列
  • 更新列
  • 更新计数器
  • 上插
  • 有效的？ 并且无效？

    在保存 Active Record 对象之前，Rails 会运行您的验证。 如果这些验证产生任何错误，Rails 不会保存对象。

    您也可以自行运行这些验证。 有效的？ 如果在对象中没有发现错误，则触发您的验证并返回 true，否则返回 false。 无效的？ 是有效的倒数吗？ 它会触发您的验证，如果在对象中发现任何错误，则返回 true，否则返回 false。

    有许多帮助方法和不同的方法来处理和显示错误消息，但我不会在这里深入研究。 随时在这里查看它们https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_record_validations.html



原文:

What is a Validation?

Here's an example.

class User < ApplicationRecord
  validates :name, presence: true
end

irb> User.create(name: "John Doe").valid?
=> true
irb> User.create(name: nil).valid?
=> false

The validation lets us know that our User is not valid without a name attribute. The second User will not be persisted to the database because it did not pass validation.

Why Use Validation?

Validations are used to make sure that only valid data is saved into your database. For example, it may be important in your application to ensure that every user provides a valid username and password. Model-level validations are the best way to ensure that only valid data is saved into your database. Validations cannot be bypassed by end users and are convenient to test and maintain. Rails provides built-in helpers for common needs, and allows you to create your own validation methods as well but I wont be covering all that here.

There are several other ways to validate data before it is saved into your database, including native database constraints, client-side validations and controller-level validations.

When Does Validation Happen?

There are two kinds of Active Record objects: those that correspond to a row inside your database and those that do not. When you create a new object, for example using the new method, that object does not belong to the database yet. Once you call save on that object it will be saved into the appropriate database table. Active Record uses the new_record? instance method to determine whether an object is already in the database or not. In this example we have an Active Record class with no validations:

irb> u = User.new(username: "Johnny")
=> #<User id: nil, username: "Johnny", created_at: nil, updated_at: nil>

irb> u.new_record?
=> true

irb> u.save
=> true

irb> u.new_record?
=> false

Creating and saving a new record will send an SQL INSERT operation to the database. Updating an existing record will send an SQL UPDATE operation instead. Validations are typically run before these commands are sent to the database. If any validations fail, the object will be marked as invalid and Active Record will not perform the INSERT or UPDATE operation. This prevents us from storing an invalid object in the database. You can choose to have specific validations run when an object is created, saved, or updated.

The following methods trigger validations, and will save the object to the database only if the object is valid:

  • create

  • create!

  • save

  • save!

  • update

  • update!

The bang operator ("!") at the end of the method raises an exception if the record is invalid. The non-bang versions don't: save and update return false, and create returns the object.

The following methods skip validations, and will save the object to the database whether it's valid or not. They should be used carefully.

  • decrement!
  • decrement_counter
  • increment!
  • increment_counter
  • insert
  • insert!
  • insert_all
  • insert_all!
  • toggle!
  • touch
  • touch_all
  • update_all
  • update_attribute
  • update_column
  • update_columns
  • update_counters
  • upsert
  • upsert_all

valid? and invalid?

Before saving an Active Record object, Rails runs your validations. If these validations produce any errors, Rails does not save the object.

You can also run these validations on your own. valid? triggers your validations and returns true if no errors were found in the object, and false otherwise. invalid? is the inverse of valid?. It triggers your validations, returning true if any errors were found in the object, and false otherwise.

There are many helper methods and different ways to deal with and display error messages but I wont delve into those here. Feel free to check them out here https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_record_validations.html

写于 2022 年 10 月 25 日

目录

前言

这个开发日志的目的是展示我如何设置我的环境来开发这个游戏。 未来的开发日志将专注于更高级别的主题，例如游戏开发更新、游戏主题和讨论等，而不是像这个开发日志这样的低级别主题。

这并不是说我不会在未来的开发日志中讨论任何细节/代码。 如果我面临一个有趣的挑战或我认为有助于解释或展示的主题，我一定会将其添加到开发日志中。

什么将用作开发机器？

选择用于创建游戏的开发机器很重要，因为它设置了游戏构建的主要目标操作系统，创建了可以使用的工具子集，并将开发环境设置为一个整体。

我的开发机器是一台 Windows 10 台式电脑。 规格如下：

  • 主板：华硕ROG STRIX Z390-E Gaming
  • 处理器：英特尔酷睿 i9 9900k
  • 内存：32GB 美商海盗船 3.0GHz DDR4
  • 贮存：
    • 三星 970 EVO Plus 500GB
    • 三星970 EVO Plus 500GB（第二款）
    • 西部数据 1TB 硬盘
  • 显卡：NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 SUPER

对于我们以这种规模进行的任何开发来说，这台机器肯定是矫枉过正，特别是因为我们正在使用 Python 开发 2-D 游戏。 这台机器对我来说也是一台游戏电脑，这就是它的规格。

由于我使用 Windows 10 作为我的开发操作系统，我们被锁定在 Windows 10 上可用的工具中。虽然这确实切断了很多 Linux 工具，但我们仍然有很多。 我们还锁定了针对游戏主要受众的 Windows 10。 归根结底，这完全没问题，因为大多数游戏都是在 Windows 上发布的，而不是其他操作系统，因此大多数游戏玩家使用 Windows 玩游戏。 所以我们的观众仍然是最大的“人群”。

这是用于游戏规划、项目文件管理和编写实际代码。

检查一切正常！

Image by storyset on Freepik

Written on October 25th, 2022

Table of Contents

Preface

The goal of this devlog is to show how I set up my environment for development of this game. Future devlogs will focus on higher level topics, such as game development updates, topics and discussion of the game, etc., instead of lower level topics like this devlog.

That is not to say I won't go into any details / code in future devlogs. If there is an interesting challenge I face or a topic I think is helpful to explain or show, I will certainly add it to the devlog.

What will be used as the development machine?

The development machine chosen to create a game is important as it sets the main target OS for the game build, creates the subset of tools that can be used, and sets the development environment as a whole.

My development machine is a Windows 10 desktop computer. The specifications are as follows:

  • Motherboard: ASUS ROG STRIX Z390-E Gaming
  • Processor: Intel Core i9 9900k
  • RAM: 32GB of CORSAIR 3.0GHz DDR4
  • Storage:
    • Samsung 970 EVO Plus 500GB
    • Samsung 970 EVO Plus 500GB (second one)
    • Western Digital 1TB Hard drive
  • Graphics Card: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 SUPER

This machine is certainly overkill for any development we are doing at this scale, especially since we are working on a 2-D game using Python. The machine also serves as a gaming computer for me, which is why these are its specifications.

Since I am using Windows 10 as my development OS, we are locked into tools that are available on Windows 10. While this does cut off a lot of Linux tools, we are still left with plenty. We are also locked into targeting Windows 10 for the main audience for the game. Python and the libraries we are using are able to run on the three major operating systems (Windows, macOS, Linux), but we will be testing and developing on the above system, so any testing on Linux and macOS will come at the end of development.

Ultimately, this is perfectly fine since most games are released on Windows versus the other operating systems and therefore most gamers use Windows to play games. So our audience is still the biggest 'chunk' of people.

Development Tool Installation / Updating

We won't be needing too many tools to get started with this project. We will install the following:

VS Code will be used as our text editor of choice. This is for planning of the game, project file management, and writing the actual code.

Aseprite will be used to create all the assets for the game, including the animations.

Tiled is our choice for taking those assets created in Aseprite and using them to create levels.

Virtual Environment

We want to keep our environment (module installations, Python version, etc.) separate from the OS wide installations. In order to do so, we need to create a separate container to store the environment. The way we will achieve this during this development run will be with Python's Virtual Environment.

It is extremely easy to setup. Ensure you have some version of Python 3+ installed on your machine, and run the following in the terminal / CMD:

In this command, we are telling Python to run the venv command, which creates a virtual environment in the current directory, and call it 'env.'

To enter the virtual environment while in the Windows OS, simply traverse to the directory containing it and enter:

Installing Libraries

To get started with the game, we don't need too many libraries. For now, we will only be using Pygame.

Checking Everything Works!

Now that we have the environment setup, all that is left to do before beginning with development is make sure it all works. We will be checking that the Python environment and Pygame installation were properly done. To accomplish this check, we will run the below Python script which opens a Pygame window:

width: int = 300
